refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods. Compare measured loss against ≤1% threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands with reliable delivery to the remote vehicle.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data to the operator with reliable delivery.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss, latency) to the operator and safety services.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement error recovery and retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) management with packet prioritization, compression, and selective retransmission for critical control/safety data.
DP-C: Establish dedicated, provisioned network link with guaranteed bandwidth and low-loss SLA from network provider.
DP-D: Implement hybrid approach combining QoS management (DP-B) with redundant channels (DP-A) and network SLA (DP-C) for defense-in-depth.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid packet loss mitigation strategy combining: (1) adaptive QoS management with prioritized retransmission for safety-critical telemetry and control commands, (2) redundant communication channels with automatic failover, and (3) negotiated network SLA with provider to guarantee baseline link quality. This approach addresses packet loss at multiple layers (application, transport, network) and aligns with high safety criticality.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation architecture (QoS logic, channel management) with network infrastructure (SLA negotiation, redundant paths). This is acceptable because packet loss is inherently a network-dependent phenomenon; decoupling would require unrealistic assumptions about network conditions.

DP-D couples safety services (critical data prioritization) with teleoperation (control/telemetry streams). This is necessary and desirable: safety-critical data must be prioritized to meet high safety criticality constraint.
DP-D does not create circular dependencies or information feedback loops that would violate independence axiom.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is justified by the nature of the problem (network quality) and safety requirements. Recommend explicit interface contracts between QoS manager, channel failover logic, and network link to minimize hidden dependencies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will define and agree on 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., 4G LTE, satellite, private network) before detailed design. Risk: Vague network assumptions lead to over/under-engineered solution.

ASSUMPTION: Redundant communication channels are technically and economically feasible (e.g., dual cellular, cellular + satellite). Risk: Cost or technical constraints may eliminate redundancy option.
ASSUMPTION: Network provider can commit to SLA with ≤1% packet loss guarantee. Risk: Provider SLA may not be achievable or may be prohibitively expensive.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive QoS and retransmission logic can be implemented without introducing unacceptable latency. Risk: Retransmission overhead may degrade control responsiveness, creating safety trade-off.
RISK: High safety criticality requires validation that packet loss mitigation does not introduce new failure modes (e.g., stale retransmitted commands, out-of-order delivery).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) prioritization rules for safety-critical vs. non-critical data, (2) maximum acceptable latency introduced by retransmission logic, (3) fallback behavior when packet loss exceeds threshold (e.g., operator alert, reduced autonomy, safe stop), and (4) network condition assumptions (latency, jitter, bandwidth ranges). This will reduce ambiguity and enable clearer IM allocation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication system, including: operator control input processing, command transmission with QoS management and retransmission logic, vehicle telemetry reception and filtering, adaptive channel failover logic, and communication quality monitoring. The module interfaces with the Vehicle (for command delivery and telemetry reception), Safety Services (for critical data prioritization and quality alerts), and Network Link (for physical transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands and mission tasking received from operator console or autonomous planner. Teleoperation module processes, prioritizes, and transmits via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sensor data, state, and status telemetry transmitted from Vehicle to operator console via Teleoperation module. Module applies QoS filtering and retransmission logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services provides critical data prioritization rules and receives communication quality degradation alerts from Teleoperation. Enables safety-aware QoS management.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical communication channel(s) managed by Teleoperation. Module implements redundant channel failover, monitors link quality, and adapts transmission strategy based on network conditions.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Assigning DP-D (packet loss mitigation) to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple sub-modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services, Network Link) without clear ownership. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns the remote equipment hardware and embedded control systems. Packet loss mitigation logic (QoS, retransmission, failover) is primarily a communication/software concern, not a vehicle hardware concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would create poor cohesion and diffuse communication logic across vehicle firmware. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of teleoperation communication quality. DP-D (QoS management, redundant channels, retransmission) is directly within the scope of teleoperation architecture. Module has clear ownership of command/telemetry transmission, channel management, and quality monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle (command delivery), Safety Services (critical data prioritization), and Network Link (physical transmission) are well-defined. Highly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and critical alerts. While Safety Services must define prioritization rules for safety-critical data, the implementation of QoS logic and retransmission mechanisms belongs in Teleoperation. Safety Services should interface with Teleoperation to provide prioritization guidance, not own the communication logic itself. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D because: (1) it owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication path (operator ↔ vehicle), (2) QoS management, retransmission, and failover logic are core teleoperation concerns, (3) the module has clear interfaces with Vehicle (command/telemetry), Safety Services (prioritization rules), and Network Link (physical transmission), and (4) assigning DP-D to Teleoperation avoids diffusion of communication logic across multiple modules.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it owns both operator-side and vehicle-side communication logic. Recommend clear separation of concerns (e.g., operator-side QoS manager vs. vehicle-side command receiver) within the module or via sub-components.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link quality and availability. If Network Link is not under Teleoperation's control, packet loss mitigation may be limited by network infrastructure constraints. Recommend explicit SLA contract with network provider and clear escalation path when SLA is violated.

RISK: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Safety Services for critical data prioritization. If prioritization rules are not clearly defined or change frequently, Teleoperation implementation may become brittle. Recommend stable interface contract with Safety Services.

RISK: Redundant channel failover logic in Teleoperation may introduce race conditions or out-of-order delivery if not carefully designed. Recommend formal verification or extensive testing of failover scenarios.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module scope to explicitly define: (1) sub-components or layers (operator-side communication manager, vehicle-side command receiver, QoS engine, failover controller), (2) internal interfaces between sub-components, (3) contract with Safety Services for critical data prioritization (e.g., list of safety-critical message types, priority levels), (4) contract with Network Link for SLA monitoring and failover triggers, and (5) error handling and fallback behavior when packet loss exceeds threshold. This will clarify module boundaries and reduce implementation risks.

fr_dp_gate.reason: FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions) is directly addressed by DP-D (hybrid packet loss mitigation combining QoS, redundancy, and SLA). DP-D is a complete and feasible solution approach. No independence axiom violations detected. Recommend proceeding to DP-IM gate.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns QoS and failover logic, but depends on Vehicle module for command delivery and on Network Link for physical transmission. Recommend explicit interface contracts to minimize hidden dependencies.

Safety Services must provide critical data prioritization rules to Teleoperation. If Safety Services requirements change, Teleoperation implementation may require rework. Recommend stable SafetyAlert interface contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score and 0.05 diffusion score. DP-D is well-contained within Teleoperation with clear external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No significant diffusion risk detected. Recommend proceeding to FR-IM gate.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-009 is verifiable via packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mock Vehicle and Network Link) or in integration with Vehicle and actual network. Recommend defining test scenarios for: (1) normal operation (packet loss <1%), (2) degraded network (packet loss >1%), (3) channel failover, (4) retransmission latency impact, and (5) safety-critical data prioritization.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-009 is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) can be further allocated to Teleoperation sub-components or design details. No ambiguity in allocation. Recommend proceeding to decomposition of sub-FRs.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify: (1) prioritization rules for safety-critical vs. non-critical data (coordinate with Safety Services), (2) maximum acceptable latency introduced by retransmission, (3) fallback behavior when packet loss exceeds threshold, (4) network condition assumptions (latency, jitter, bandwidth ranges).

Refine Teleoperation module scope to define: (1) sub-components (operator-side manager, vehicle-side receiver, QoS engine, failover controller), (2) internal interfaces, (3) contract with Safety Services for prioritization, (4) contract with Network Link for SLA monitoring.

Define test plan for FR-NGHE-009 verification, including: (1) packet loss measurement methodology, (2) test scenarios (normal, degraded, failover), (3) acceptance criteria, (4) safety-critical data validation.
Establish SLA contract with network provider specifying: (1) baseline packet loss guarantee, (2) latency and jitter bounds, (3) failover response time, (4) escalation and remediation procedures.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) address distinct concerns: (1) command transmission reliability, (2) telemetry transmission reliability, (3) quality monitoring and alerting, (4) error recovery. Each sub-FR should be decomposed to DP and IM levels to clarify design decisions and implementation responsibilities. Proceeding with decomposition will reduce design risk and improve clarity for implementation teams.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands with reliable delivery to the remote vehicle (target: command delivery success rate ≥99%, latency <X ms).
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data to the operator with reliable delivery (target: telemetry delivery success rate ≥99%, update frequency ≥Y Hz).

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss, latency) to the operator and safety services (target: detection latency <Z ms, alert accuracy ≥95%).

FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement error recovery and retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact (target: recovery time <W ms, retransmission overhead <V% of bandwidth).

revise.why: DP-D should be refined to specify: (1) prioritization rules for safety-critical vs. non-critical data (coordinate with Safety Services), (2) maximum acceptable latency introduced by retransmission (to avoid control responsiveness degradation), (3) fallback behavior when packet loss exceeds threshold (e.g., operator alert, reduced autonomy, safe stop), (4) network condition assumptions (latency, jitter, bandwidth ranges). This will enable clearer allocation of sub-DPs to sub-FRs and reduce implementation ambiguity.

assumptions: Customer will define and agree on 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., 4G LTE, satellite, private network) before detailed design.
Redundant communication channels are technically and economically feasible (e.g., dual cellular, cellular + satellite).
Network provider can commit to SLA with ≤1% packet loss guarantee.
Adaptive QoS and retransmission logic can be implemented without introducing unacceptable latency (latency budget to be defined).
Safety Services will provide stable interface contract for critical data prioritization rules.
Vehicle module will provide reliable command delivery interface to Teleoperation module.
Network Link module will provide SLA monitoring and failover capability to Teleoperation module.
open_risks: RISK: Vague network assumptions lead to over/under-engineered solution. Mitigation: Define agreed network conditions early with customer.
RISK: Cost or technical constraints may eliminate redundancy option. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study for redundant channels.
RISK: Network provider SLA may not be achievable or may be prohibitively expensive. Mitigation: Negotiate SLA early; identify alternative network providers.
RISK: Retransmission overhead may degrade control responsiveness, creating safety trade-off. Mitigation: Define latency budget; validate via simulation and testing.

RISK: Packet loss mitigation may introduce new failure modes (e.g., stale retransmitted commands, out-of-order delivery). Mitigation: Formal verification of failover logic; extensive testing of edge cases.
RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and complex. Mitigation: Define clear sub-components and internal interfaces.
RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link quality; packet loss mitigation may be limited by network infrastructure. Mitigation: Establish explicit SLA contract with network provider.
evidence_needed: Customer agreement on 'agreed network conditions' (latency, jitter, bandwidth, availability).
Feasibility study for redundant communication channels (technical and cost analysis).
Network provider SLA proposal with packet loss, latency, and jitter guarantees.
Latency budget for teleoperation system (operator input → vehicle response).
Safety analysis of packet loss mitigation mechanisms (FMEA, fault tree analysis).
Detailed design of Teleoperation module sub-components and internal interfaces.
Test plan for FR-NGHE-009 verification (packet loss measurement, failover scenarios, safety-critical data validation).
Formal verification or extensive testing of failover logic (race conditions, out-of-order delivery).
